pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acquired Urea Cycle Disorder (UCD) precipitated by drug interaction, specifically Valproic Acid (Valproate) Induced Hyperammonemia in an individual with potentially partial, underlying Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC) Deficiency. THE SMOKING GUN: A secondary, highly detailed medication history was obtained from the fiancée, revealing the patient had recently been prescribed Valproic Acid (Depakote) 500mg BID, initiated one week prior for presumed migraine prophylaxis by an outside PCP following the initial viral illness. This was missed on the initial ED screen which focused only on common illicit substances. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: The Urea Cycle is the primary mechanism for detoxifying nitrogenous waste (ammonia) into urea. Even partial, subclinical defects in one of the cycle’s enzymes—most commonly Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC)—can remain asymptomatic until the system is stressed. Valproic Acid (VPA) is a potent inhibitor of Carbamoyl Phosphate Synthetase I (CPS I) and N-acetylglutamate synthase (NAGS) in the liver mitochondria, two critical enzymes in the urea cycle. When VPA is introduced, it acutely impairs the already compromised urea cycle capacity, leading to a catastrophic breakdown of ammonia clearance. This acute, uncompensated metabolic stress results in massive, non-hepatic-failure-related hyperammonemia, rapid neurotoxicity, and cerebral herniation risk if not immediately treated with aggressive ammonia scavenging and potentially continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT).
